introSection: Texas Cyclone was a wooden roller coaster located at Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA. It operated from 1976 to 2005.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The park along with its then general manager Bill Crandall had hoped to purchase the original Cyclone from Coney Island, but this fell through. The park (under Bill Crandall) eventually decided to build their own model that was taller and faster.

Early construction started in summer 1975, with concrete pilings being installed. On December 12, 1975, the construction of lumber for the Texas Cyclone started.

On January 7, 1976, high winds toppled over the incomplete "North Bend" portion of the structure, which had been under construction for five weeks. The damaged caused was replaced within short of three weeks, despite construction being eight weeks behind. The ride's planned April opening was delayed until June.

Texas Cyclone opened on June 12, 1976 as part of a new Coney Island themed area, which also contained International Plaza. The ride's trains were originally provided by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters. Later that same year, seatbelts would be added to the last car, with all cars receiving this in 1977.

In 1978, seat dividers were added to Texas Cyclone's trains.

In 1979, Texas Cyclone was re-profiled, with the track on the ride's south bend lowered by two feet to ensure that the train does not stall in high winds. The coaster also received a new brake system.

In 1981, Texas Cyclone's original PTC trains were changed following a injury lawsuit.

In 1982, Texas Cyclone's three" and five" wide track rails were replaced with six" wide track rails. The following year, its transfer track was modified following an employee death.

In 1985, Texas Cyclone received a new brake system.

In 1986, the ride received new air operated gates in its queue.

In 1987, Texas Cyclone's transfer track was rebuilt as a complete loop. Also that same year, the ride was retrofitted with new Morgan "Coffin Car" trains after another injury lawsuit.

In 1991, Texas Cyclone is re-profiled once again, with its north bend smoothened out. The lapbar straps were also removed.

In 1996, to celebrate its 20th anniversary, the park reduced its first drop by six feet.

In 2001, Texas Cyclone celebrated its 25th Anniversary. For its 25th birthday, the Morgan trains had their oversized headrests removed and were completely rebuilt.

In 2002, Texas Cyclone's handrails along its track were repainted an deep red colour. The "gravity hill" was also retracked and the ride received a new automation system.

The ride closed permanently with Six Flags AstroWorld on October 30, 2005 and was subsequently demolished on March 9, 2006.

Shortly after the park's closure, a short section of track from the Texas Cyclone was preserved and put into storage. In November 2020, the section of track was transported to the National Roller Coaster Museum for display. Larson International assisted with the move.

The trains from Texas Cyclone were transported to La Ronde for use as spare parts for Le Monstre.

The front lead car of the red train was later sold on to a coffee shop in Montreal, Canada called La Graine Brûlée where it is still present there today.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On July 29, 1983, a park employee was killed and others were injured during a test run of the coaster when a train was loaded in the transfer track position. The manager responsible forgot to move the train back to its running position. The train was loaded up with employees and ended up venturing off towards the end of the work track crashing into the storage house.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The layout was a mirror-image of that of the original Cyclone at Coney Island. However, Texas Cyclone was taller and longer.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The former rolling stock was replaced with new trains from Morgan in 1987.

fullText:
Texas Cyclone was a wooden roller coaster located at Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA. It operated from 1976 to 2005.

The park along with its then general manager Bill Crandall had hoped to purchase the original Cyclone from Coney Island, but this fell through. The park (under Bill Crandall) eventually decided to build their own model that was taller and faster.

Early construction started in summer 1975, with concrete pilings being installed. On December 12, 1975, the construction of lumber for the Texas Cyclone started.

On January 7, 1976, high winds toppled over the incomplete "North Bend" portion of the structure, which had been under construction for five weeks. The damaged caused was replaced within short of three weeks, despite construction being eight weeks behind. The ride's planned April opening was delayed until June.

Texas Cyclone opened on June 12, 1976 as part of a new Coney Island themed area, which also contained International Plaza. The ride's trains were originally provided by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters. Later that same year, seatbelts would be added to the last car, with all cars receiving this in 1977.

In 1978, seat dividers were added to Texas Cyclone's trains.

In 1979, Texas Cyclone was re-profiled, with the track on the ride's south bend lowered by two feet to ensure that the train does not stall in high winds. The coaster also received a new brake system.

In 1981, Texas Cyclone's original PTC trains were changed following a injury lawsuit.

In 1982, Texas Cyclone's three" and five" wide track rails were replaced with six" wide track rails. The following year, its transfer track was modified following an employee death.

In 1985, Texas Cyclone received a new brake system.

In 1986, the ride received new air operated gates in its queue.

In 1987, Texas Cyclone's transfer track was rebuilt as a complete loop. Also that same year, the ride was retrofitted with new Morgan "Coffin Car" trains after another injury lawsuit.

In 1991, Texas Cyclone is re-profiled once again, with its north bend smoothened out. The lapbar straps were also removed.

In 1996, to celebrate its 20th anniversary, the park reduced its first drop by six feet.

In 2001, Texas Cyclone celebrated its 25th Anniversary. For its 25th birthday, the Morgan trains had their oversized headrests removed and were completely rebuilt.

In 2002, Texas Cyclone's handrails along its track were repainted an deep red colour. The "gravity hill" was also retracked and the ride received a new automation system.

The ride closed permanently with Six Flags AstroWorld on October 30, 2005 and was subsequently demolished on March 9, 2006.

Shortly after the park's closure, a short section of track from the Texas Cyclone was preserved and put into storage. In November 2020, the section of track was transported to the National Roller Coaster Museum for display. Larson International assisted with the move.

The trains from Texas Cyclone were transported to La Ronde for use as spare parts for Le Monstre.

The front lead car of the red train was later sold on to a coffee shop in Montreal, Canada called La Graine Brûlée where it is still present there today.

On July 29, 1983, a park employee was killed and others were injured during a test run of the coaster when a train was loaded in the transfer track position. The manager responsible forgot to move the train back to its running position. The train was loaded up with employees and ended up venturing off towards the end of the work track crashing into the storage house.

The layout was a mirror-image of that of the original Cyclone at Coney Island. However, Texas Cyclone was taller and longer.

Unpainted track and white supports.

The former rolling stock was replaced with new trains from Morgan in 1987.
